overview: A day after the first American combat deaths of the renewed war, the fight is visibly widening. Israel warned it would answer 'with full force' if Iranian missiles hit its territory — threatening to reopen the front that has stayed dormant since fighting reignited on 8 July and turn a US-Iran duel back into the multi-front regional war of the spring. On the ground the exchanges kept spreading: the US struck Iran for an eighth night to 'swiftly punish' the two troops killed at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase, while Iran's return fire set a Kuwaiti power-and-water plant ablaze — its second such strike in two days — and sirens sounded in Bahrain and reportedly Jordan. With Tehran having called off the interim deal and the war now the least popular in the history of US polling, both sides are lurching toward the all-out fight each said it wanted to avoid.

keyPoints.text: Two US service members were killed and one left missing at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase — the first American combat deaths of the renewed war and the red line Trump had set.
Israel threatened to respond 'with full force' if Iranian missiles strike its territory, raising the prospect that the sidelined Israeli front reopens into a wider regional war.

The US struck Iran an eighth straight night to 'swiftly punish' the troop deaths; Iran returned fire across the Gulf and says it hit US forces in Kuwait, with a fire at a Kuwaiti power-and-water plant.
Iran's strikes on US-aligned states widened: Kuwait reported a second power-and-water plant hit in two days, sirens sounded in Bahrain and reportedly Jordan.
Tehran hardened its rupture with Washington, calling off the interim deal and threatening 'unforgettable lessons'; Iranian outlets put July's toll near 50.
The conflict is now the least popular US war since modern polling began, per The Economist — a domestic bind for Trump just as the first American troops die.

watch.text: Whether Israel makes good on its 'full force' threat and rejoins the war, or Iran tests it with a strike on Israeli soil — either of which would reopen the multi-front regional conflict.
The fate of the one US service member listed missing after the Jordan strike, and whether fresh American deaths push Washington toward the wider campaign it was reported weighing.
Tehran's warning of 'unforgettable lessons' and that 'no political border' is safe, tested against continued strikes on Gulf allies hosting US forces.

stories.title: Iranian missiles kill US troops in Jordan, crossing Trump's red line
stories.summary: Two US service members were killed and one left missing when Iranian ballistic missiles struck Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase, US Central Command said — the first American combat deaths since the war reignited on 8 July. CENTCOM said the troops were killed 'while partner forces defended against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks'; Al Jazeera published video it said captured the moment a missile hit the base. President Trump called the deaths 'a very sad thing.' The strike lands squarely on the threshold the White House had treated as its red line — direct US casualties — and, as Fortune framed it, marks the point at which Iran 'crossed Trump's red line for resuming all-out war.' Senator Mark Warner, the Virginia Democrat who vice-chairs the Senate Intelligence Committee, called it 'remarkable in many ways' that more US troops have not been killed given how exposed American forces are across the region. The New York Times reported Jordan, long a quiet US partner, is now becoming a central front in the conflict.
stories.explainer: Through the first week of renewed fighting, both sides calibrated around a tacit line: Iran hit US bases and allies but stopped short of killing Americans, letting Washington answer with infrastructure strikes rather than an all-out campaign. Dead US soldiers remove that off-ramp. Every prior US war of the century — the Beirut barracks, the Jordan drone strike of 2024 — shows how a mass-casualty event against US troops forces a president toward escalation for domestic reasons alone, regardless of strategy. That is why the Jordan strike is more dangerous than any single night of bombing: it converts a contained duel into a question of American prestige.

stories.title: Israel threatens to rejoin the war 'with full force'
stories.summary: Israel warned it would retaliate against Iran 'with full force' if Tehran's missiles strike within its borders, The Guardian reported, threatening to reopen a front that has been largely dormant since the June memorandum. Israel — which with the United States launched the war's opening blow, Operation Epic Fury, in late February — had stayed on the sidelines through the renewed fighting that began on 8 July, leaving the current phase a US-Iran duel focused on the Strait of Hormuz. The threat lands as Iranian missiles and drones range across the Gulf and Washington weighs a wider campaign. A direct Iranian strike on Israel, or an Israeli entry, would convert the two-party war back into the multi-front regional conflict of the spring, when Hezbollah's Lebanon front and Gulf-capital strikes were all live at once.
stories.explainer: The renewed war since 8 July has been narrower than the spring's: fought mainly between US and Iranian forces over Hormuz, with Israel absent. Israel rejoining is the single fastest route to a wider war, because it doubles the fronts Iran must fight and risks reactivating Hezbollah and the other 'Axis of Resistance' militias along Israel's borders. That is why a conditional Israeli threat — 'full force' if hit — matters even before a shot is fired: it hands Iran a lever (strike Israel to widen the war and split US attention) and hands Israel a tripwire that a single stray missile could trigger.

stories.title: US strikes Iran an eighth night to 'swiftly punish' troop deaths
stories.summary: Within hours of the Jordan strike, the US launched a new wave of attacks on Iran — the eighth consecutive night — in what the Guardian and NPR reported was framed as an effort to 'swiftly punish' Iran for the two soldiers killed, hitting the Sirik and Hajiabad areas of Hormozgan province. Iran returned fire on US positions across the Gulf, saying it launched drones at the US military in Kuwait, according to the BBC, and Iranian officials said US strikes had hit an under-construction nuclear power plant. The exchange marks a sharp acceleration of a campaign that had already pushed from military targets into bridges, power and water infrastructure across Iran's south, and comes as Iran's own accounts put the July death toll at roughly 50.
stories.explainer: A retaliation explicitly labelled 'punishment' rather than 'degrading military capability' is a rhetorical shift that matters: it reframes the strikes from a limited campaign with a military objective into a spiral of reprisal, where each side must answer the last blow to preserve credibility. Tit-for-tat punishment is the mechanism by which limited wars become total ones, because neither side can be seen to absorb a hit without responding — and Iran's decision to answer the eighth night by striking US forces in Kuwait shows the loop already turning.

stories.title: Iran calls off the interim deal, threatens 'unforgettable lessons'
stories.summary: Tehran hardened its formal rupture with Washington, calling off the interim peace agreement and warning of 'unforgettable lessons,' according to CNBC, after saying earlier that the June memorandum of understanding is now 'suspended.' The move builds on Iran's declaration a day earlier that it was suspending its commitments to the interim deal, and reaches back to the nuclear file the June framework had tried to freeze. It comes as Iranian outlets put the toll of July's US strikes at around 50 killed. Cutting the other way, UN Secretary-General António Guterres — the world body's top official — has called for a new diplomatic push after strikes on civilian areas, and US Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Lebanon's president in Washington, but with US troops now dead and Iran threatening reprisal, the thread of negotiation has frayed to near nothing.
stories.explainer: The interim deal was the last guardrail over the war's nuclear dimension: a set of mutual commitments that, however frayed, gave both sides something to point to. Formally calling it off — not merely suspending commitments — removes that guardrail just as IAEA inspectors are already locked out, leaving neither a live agreement nor independent monitoring over Iran's most sensitive facilities. A combatant that discards its nuclear commitments mid-war, while its soldiers are killing Americans, is signaling it may reopen enrichment or expel the last observers — the escalation that most alarms outside powers, because it turns a conventional war into a proliferation crisis.

stories.title: Iran's fire spreads across US Gulf allies; Kuwait plant ablaze
stories.summary: Iran's retaliation kept spreading across the US-aligned states that ring it rather than onto American soil directly. Al Jazeera reported Kuwait and Bahrain came under several rounds of Iranian missile and drone attacks, with a fire breaking out at a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant — the second such strike on Kuwaiti infrastructure in two days — and sirens reportedly sounding in Jordan, where the fatal strike on Muwaffaq Salti Airbase landed. The pattern matches Iran's threat, reported by The Hill, that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe. A leaked US assessment concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign — evidence it can sustain a regional fight — and its drone strikes on US forces in Kuwait show the willingness to keep hitting American troops wherever they are based.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the Gulf states that shelter its bases raises the cost of the American campaign while pressuring those governments to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. Striking power and water plants in particular targets the states' civilian lifelines, not just US installations — a way to make hosting American forces politically unbearable. It is also the exact mechanism by which a US-Iran duel becomes a regional war: the Jordan strike proves the danger is no longer hypothetical, and each hit on a neighbour's infrastructure drags that country closer to full belligerency.

stories.title: The war narrows around Hormuz as fuel prices climb
stories.summary: With the Strait of Hormuz closed since 12 July and US forces enforcing a counter-blockade — disabling tankers from the air and boarding the M/T Wen Yao — the conflict has increasingly centered on the waterway itself, described by NBC as a war now 'all about the Strait of Hormuz.' NPR reported that recognizing Iran's claim over the strait could set a dangerous precedent for other contested waterways. The energy shock is biting at home: US diesel has topped $5 a gallon and regular gasoline is nearing $4, according to AAA figures cited by the Guardian — roughly $1.25 higher than a year ago — and Iran has threatened to halt all Middle East energy exports if the blockade holds.
stories.explainer: About a fifth of the world's oil moves through Hormuz, a chokepoint so narrow that a few small islands sit astride its lanes, which is why closing it produced the largest supply disruption in the history of the oil market. The fear NPR flags is precedent: if force can convert a country's coastline into control of an international strait, other powers may be tempted to do the same at chokepoints like Malacca or Bab-el-Mandeb.

stories.title: The Iran war is now America's least popular since polling began
stories.summary: The renewed Iran war is now the least popular US conflict since modern polling began, The Economist reported, with public support falling below even the low points of the Iraq and Vietnam wars. The finding lands as American troops die in the conflict for the first time and as The Guardian asks why Trump is 'risking midterm disaster' by resuming an unpopular war. AP reported that 16 US service members have died in the Iran war overall, in a conflict fought largely in the air, and Senator Mark Warner said it was 'remarkable in many ways' there had not been more, given how exposed US forces are across the region.
stories.explainer: Domestic unpopularity is a strategic variable, not just a political one: a president fighting a war the public rejects has less room to escalate and more incentive to seek an exit — but dead soldiers pull the other way, creating pressure to 'finish' the fight rather than be seen abandoning it. The tension between an unpopular war and a red line just crossed is exactly the bind Washington now faces, and with midterm elections approaching it sharpens by the week.

stories.title: US surges warplanes and tightens the blockade
stories.summary: The Pentagon has reinforced the region as the war reignites. Air & Space Forces Magazine reported the US deploying F-16 and F-35 fighter jets to the Middle East, and Axios reported Washington sending dozens of aerial-refueling planes to Israel as Iranian escalation loomed. The buildup gives concrete shape to the Wall Street Journal's report that Trump was leaning toward expanding US operations against Iran — an option that becomes far more likely now that Iranian missiles have killed American troops. Refueling tankers in particular are the enabler for sustained long-range air operations, so their forward deployment reads as preparation for a heavier, longer campaign rather than the current nightly strikes.
stories.explainer: Force posture often signals intent more reliably than statements. Fifth-generation F-35s add stealth penetration against Iran's surviving air defenses, while a fleet of tankers extends how far and how long any strike package can operate — the difference between harassing raids and a campaign able to hold targets deep inside Iran at will. Positioned now, alongside the reimposed port blockade and the first US combat deaths, they suggest Washington has the pieces in place for a wider war should it choose to fight one.

stories.title: Iraq courts Washington with a Hormuz workaround
stories.summary: Iraq signed 48 deals with US companies during a visit by its prime minister to Washington, Al Jazeera reported, in a rare piece of constructive economic news amid the war. Among them is an agreement to rebuild the long-defunct Iraq-Syria crude oil pipeline, a route that could carry oil to the Mediterranean and bypass the Strait of Hormuz entirely. For Baghdad — which shares a long border with Iran and hosts US forces Iran has threatened — the deals are a bet on staying aligned with Washington while insulating its own oil exports from the chokepoint at the center of the war.
stories.explainer: Every barrel that can leave the region without passing through Hormuz weakens Iran's central form of leverage. A working Iraq-Syria pipeline would give Gulf-adjacent crude an alternative artery to the Mediterranean, which is why such projects — dormant for years — suddenly attract investment the moment the strait is closed: the war has made bypassing Hormuz worth almost any price.

stories.title: Nuclear dimension lingers at Bushehr
stories.summary: Satellite imagery published last week showed fresh damage inside Bushehr, Iran's largest nuclear power plant, from strikes dated between 7 and 12 July — the window in which the ceasefire collapsed — and on 19 July Iranian officials said US strikes had also hit an under-construction nuclear power plant. With IAEA safeguards access suspended since strikes on Natanz earlier in the war, and Iran now calling off its interim-deal commitments, outside monitors are left reliant on satellites to gauge the condition of sensitive facilities. As US strikes push deeper into Iran's south — Bushehr sits on the Gulf coast near the newly targeted infrastructure — the risk of damage to a fueled reactor remains a background hazard to the whole campaign.
stories.explainer: Bushehr is a civilian power reactor, not an enrichment site, so the danger near it is radiological rather than proliferation: damage to a fueled reactor's cooling or containment could release contamination across the Gulf regardless of who is blamed. With inspectors locked out, satellite analysis is the only independent window into whether the war is edging toward a nuclear-safety crisis.

stories.title: Detainee off-ramp stays in dispute
stories.summary: The release of Dena Karari, a dual US-Iranian citizen detained since December 2024, which President Trump announced on 15 July as a possible opening, remains in dispute even as both sides step up attacks. With Iran now calling off its interim-deal commitments, declaring wartime negotiation 'over,' and its missiles having killed US troops, the thin channel of indirect contacts the release briefly seemed to open has all but closed. Separately, the family of Craig Foreman, a Briton jailed in Iran, said his sentence had been extended by two years for speaking to the media from prison.
stories.explainer: Detainee releases are normally the lowest-cost trust signal in US-Iran diplomacy, which is why a contested one matters: if the sides cannot even agree on whether a hand-over happened, the gesture that read as a path to climbdown loses its meaning — and dead American soldiers drain what little remained of it.

stories.title: Lebanon presses to disarm Hezbollah
stories.summary: Lebanon's foreign minister, Youssef Raggi, said the government had decided to 'end Hezbollah's military presence,' stressing that decisions on war and foreign policy are the 'exclusive prerogative of the Lebanese state.' The move to assert a state monopoly on force and disarm the Iran-backed group comes as Beirut tries to stay out of the widening US-Iran war — US Secretary of State Marco Rubio met Lebanon's president in Washington on 19 July — and after the 2026 Lebanon war displaced more than a sixth of the country's population.
stories.explainer: Hezbollah is the most powerful member of Iran's 'Axis of Resistance,' the network of allied militias Tehran uses to project force. A Lebanese push to disarm it would strip Iran of its strongest regional lever just as Tehran most needs to threaten escalation — though forcing the issue risks reopening Lebanon's own internal conflict, and Israel's fresh 'full force' threat raises the stakes on whether the Lebanon front stays quiet.

notes: Briefing synthesized from the day's wire feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-19T18:07Z by the scheduled GitHub Actions workflow). The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was again blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this briefing draws on the workflow-committed feed rather than a fresh manual pull. Since the earlier 19 July briefing, the war has widened on three fronts: Israel threatened to rejoin the fight 'with full force' if Iranian missiles hit its territory; Iran's strikes on US Gulf allies set a Kuwaiti power-and-water plant ablaze (a second such hit in two days); and The Economist reported the conflict is now the least popular US war since modern polling began, as AP counted 16 total US service-member deaths. These build on the day's lead development — the first US combat deaths of the renewed war at Jordan's Muwaffaq Salti Airbase and an eighth 'punishment' night of US strikes. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — casualty figures, the '70%' missile-retention estimate, the AP death tally, and Iranian-reported strikes — are attributed to their sources; the one missing US service member's status was unresolved at the time of writing.
